manipulated_prompt_obj.content:
You are playing a negotiation game in which you have to agree on how to divide a set of items among you and another player.

Rules:
(a) You and the other player are given a set of items. Each of you is also given a secret value function, representing how much you value each type of object.
(b) You exchange messages with the other player to agree on who gets which items. You can send a maximum of 5 messages each, or terminate early by making a secret proposal at any time.
(c) You are each asked to submit a secret proposal indicating the items you want formatted in square brackets as follows: "[Proposal: <number> <object name>, <number> <object name>, <...>]"
(d) If your proposals are complementary, i.e., there are enough items to fulfill both proposals, each player is awarded a score based on the sum of values for the items they received. Otherwise, both of you get zero points.
(e) Your goal is to maximize the score you receive.

Let us start.

The set of available items is:

1 pot, 1 candy, 1 whistle, 2 dice

Your secret value function is:

pot: 2, candy: 3, whistle: 3, dice: 1

IMPORTANT: Your messages, unless it is the secret proposal, are directly transmitted to the other player, so do not include any response to the rules or text announcing your message. To make a secret proposal, use the indicated format. Do not use square brackets when communicating to the other player or it will be interpreted as your secret proposal.

message.content:
I'll take the candy, whistle, pot, and one die. Could you take the other die?

[Proposal: 1 candy, 1 whistle, 1 pot, 1 die]
message.reasoning:
We need to parse the game. We have a set of items:

- 1 pot
- 1 candy
- 1 whistle
- 2 dice

The user has a secret value function (which we can see):

- pot: 2
- candy: 3
- whistle: 3
- dice: 1

We must negotiate with the other player (the opponent). The opponent's value function is secret to us but maybe we can guess. The rules say each is given a secret value function, representing how much they value each type of object. So we don't know theirs; we have to exchange messages to agree on allocation.

We can exchange messages, maximum of 5 messages each, or terminate early by making a secret proposal at any time.

We can send messages in plain text (not secret). These messages are directly transmitted to the other player. The goal is to maximize your own score (the sum of values for the items you get). The secret proposals must be formatted as: "[Proposal: <number> <object name>, <number> <object name>, <...>]".

If proposals are complementary (i.e., there are enough items to fulfill both proposals), each gets score based on sum of values for items they receive. Otherwise both get zero.

So we must propose items we want. The other player will also propose. If the total items required in both proposals do not exceed available items (i.e., can be satisfied), then each gets the sum of values for the items they requested (the items they get). They cannot request the same item twice. Each item is unique (though there are multiple dice). So the proposals must specify counts.

We can also negotiate using messages: "I want the candy", etc. The other player might accept or propose something. At any point, we can also submit a secret proposal. The final step is that after we have exchanged proposals? We need to think about the game flow. Typically each player can make a secret proposal in the final step. But the description says "you can send a maximum of 5 messages each, or terminate early by making a secret proposal at any time." That suggests that after up to 5 messages (including possibly a secret proposal?), you can at any point make the secret proposal and end the negotiation. Once secret proposals are both submitted (or maybe each submits one secret proposal at the same time?), the game checks if they are complementary. But we need to confirm the exact mechanics.

We have to consider that there are two players; each can send up to 5 messages; at any time they can decide to send a secret proposal using the format. Once a secret proposal is sent, presumably the game ends and both have submitted proposals? Or maybe you can send secret proposal only as a final step? The description: "you can send a maximum of 5 messages each, or terminate early by making a secret proposal at any time." Means that each side can either keep sending messages up to the limit, or they can prematurely end the negotiation by sending a secret proposal (I think only one player needs to send the secret proposal; but if one sends secret proposal, presumably the other also must respond with secret proposal or maybe the game ends with both proposals?). However the rule (d) says: "If your proposals are complementary, i.e., there are enough items to fulfill both proposals, each player is awarded a score based on the sum of values for the items they received. Otherwise, both of you get zero points."

Thus there are "your proposals" - presumably both your secret proposals. So each player must submit a secret proposal. It seems both must be submitted, but they can be done in any order. In practice the negotiation ends when both have made secret proposals. But they may have to be submitted in a certain "secret" format (so the other does not see the details until both have submitted? But the format includes square brackets which are not to be used in regular messages.)

We need to maximize our own points; we know our value function but not theirs. But we can try to infer based on typical valuations? The set includes items with known values to us. So maximum we can get is 10 if we get all items. But we have to share with the other player; they must get some too. Our goal is to maximize our own. Since we don't know theirs, but they may want something else.

We also want to propose something that is feasible: the total number of each item must be enough. Our secret proposal is for a set of items we want. The other will also propose.
